understanding.goal_understanding: The goal of the game was to solve each level’s puzzle by manipulating selectable pieces and pushable blocks so that the level’s win condition was satisfied—typically by getting at least one pushable object (or in some cases a specific selectable piece) onto the goal tile(s) marked with '+', at which point the engine reports WIN. Clearing all levels (or reaching a WIN state in the final available level) constituted winning the overall run.
understanding.mechanics_understanding: Movement was grid-based, with positions given in pixel coordinates that mapped to cells (3 pixels per cell). Issuing MOVE_UP, DOWN, LEFT, or RIGHT attempted to shift the currently selected piece by one cell-equivalent (3 pixels) in that direction. If a pushable block was immediately adjacent in the direction of movement and there was free space beyond it, moving into it would push the block along with the selected piece. If an object or boundary behaved as a wall, it could block movement entirely or in specific ways, sometimes requiring experimentation, even when the heuristic movement.<dir>_blocked flag was only an indication of adjacency rather than a guarantee of failure. Multiple selectable pieces could exist; using CLICK with a target_position that matched another selectable’s coordinates instantly switched control to that piece. The semantic_grid provided an abstract map (P for the current piece, S for other selectables, B for blocks, # for walls, + for goals, . for empty) to plan routes and pushes under a limited per-level step budget, which decreased by one with each action (MOVE or CLICK) until reaching zero, at which point the run ended if WIN had not yet been achieved.
